brief_summary: Annotated cross-section showing the Nova Socket hot-swap footprint on a PCB. Left half seats a standard mechanical switch with four contact legs meeting copper pads; right half seats a TMR magnetic switch with a stem-mounted magnet over a sensor pad, in an identically-dimensioned dashed footprint outline. A coral bracket spans both halves to make the article's central claim legible: one socket, one PCB, the switch technology is decided per key at assembly, not at manufacture.
data_sources: apps/web/src/content/articles/keychron-nova-socket-hybrid.mdx:What Nova Socket actually changes — 'The socket footprint is wired to support a mechanical switch's contact-closure legs and a TMR switch's magnet-over-sensor geometry at the same time, in the same four-pin outline. Seat a standard MX-compatible mechanical switch and the board reads a simple make/break. Seat a TMR switch and the same socket reads analog travel'

apps/web/src/content/articles/keychron-nova-socket-hybrid.mdx:frontmatter heroImageAlt — 'Overhead illustration of a keyboard hot-swap socket footprint split down the middle, one half seated with a standard mechanical switch stem, the other half seated with a magnet-and-sensor TMR switch stem, both sharing the same four-pin socket outline.'

apps/web/src/content/articles/keychron-nova-socket-hybrid.mdx:What Nova Socket actually changes — 'It's not a new switch. It's a socket that refuses to make the switch-technology decision on the buyer's behalf.'
